title: C'mon C'mon
review:
From its first scene, C'mon C'mon suggests a filmmaker working with a clear, persuasive idea of tone. As a drama from Mike Mills, it is especially alert to mood: how a room sounds before an argument, how a close-up can either reveal or conceal, how a cut placed half a second earlier changes the emotional temperature of a scene. The leads understand how to play subtext rather than merely announce it, and that matters because this is not a picture built on plot twists alone. It asks the audience to notice behavior, contradiction, and the way people perform certainty when certainty is the very thing they lack.

The direction favors clarity over fuss. The screenplay is strongest when it lets motives remain slightly unsettled Characters are allowed to arrive in layers, which gives the film a welcome density. It understands when to accelerate and when to sit with a moment. Visual design does a great deal of narrative heavy lifting, turning even transitional scenes into opportunities for emphasis rather than dead air. The music adds shape to scenes that might otherwise play too plainly without trying to bully the viewer into feeling more than the material has earned.

What finally distinguishes C'mon C'mon is the seriousness with which it treats tone. It wants comedy, melancholy, dread, or excitement not as isolated effects but as part of a single design, and it succeeds often enough to keep the enterprise engaging. The film works because its style and substance are pulling in the same direction. What lingers is not just craftsmanship but feeling: the sense of a movie that has earned its final notes.

title: Barbie
review:
Barbie mistakes noise for momentum and scale for conviction. As a fantasy comedy from Greta Gerwig, it is especially alert to mood: how a room sounds before an argument, how a close-up can either reveal or conceal, how a cut placed half a second earlier changes the emotional temperature of a scene. It asks the audience to notice behavior, contradiction, and the way people perform certainty when certainty is the very thing they lack.

The direction favors clarity over fuss, but control on the surface is not the same thing as dramatic pressure. The screenplay sometimes underlines its points, though it also knows how to build a scene, yet the dialogue often spells out tensions that should have been dramatized. The movie keeps stopping to explain itself just when it ought to be building momentum. Cinematography gives the film much of its tactile appeal, though visual intelligence can only compensate so much when scenes flatten instead of build. Music knows when to push and when to hold back, but it cannot rescue moments that have not been properly prepared.

What finally distinguishes Barbie is the seriousness with which it treats tone. It wants comedy, melancholy, dread, or excitement not as isolated effects but as part of a single design, and it succeeds often enough to keep the enterprise engaging. Too often, the film confuses emphasis with insight, and spectacle with dramatic accumulation. What remains is a collection of isolated virtues in search of a coherent movie.
